message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs. It also includes 3,044 annotated sentences in 100 dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that first separates on-task and off-task intents, then defines task-specific on-task intents and universal off-task dialog-act categories. Off-task intents are shared across tasks and include six general intents (open question, yes/no question, positive answer, negative answer, responsive statement, nonresponsive statement) and six social intents (greeting, closing, apology, thanking, respond to thank, and hold), and annotation is done at the sentence level after segmenting each conversation turn into single sentences.
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

The model is evaluated with automatic metrics: Perplexity (PPL), Response-Intent Prediction (RIP), Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP), and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP); RIP/RSP compare predicted response intents/slots against human-annotated ground truth, while ERIP/ERSP additionally score whether the predicted intent/slot is within the set of acceptable outputs using transition probabilities estimated from training data. Human evaluation uses five criteria: fluency, coherence, engagement, dialog length, and task success score, where task success is measured by the average types of attacker information obtained (name, address, phone number).
Unanswerable

They compare against two state-of-the-art early rumour detection baselines: Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012). Both use message-, user-, topic-, and propagation-based features with an SVM classifier; Yang operates with a 24-hour delay, while Liu is claimed to work in real time but requires a cluster of 5 repeated messages.
Accuracy and the standard TDT evaluation procedure using Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves with the official TDT3 evaluation scripts. They also measure efficiency by throughput per second.

Yes. Their method is supervised: they frame rumour detection as a classification problem, use an SVM, and learn the weight vector and detection threshold on a training set; the pseudo-feedback feature is also trained in two rounds using SVMs.
Yes. They created their own dataset, including 202 confirmed rumours and 202 non-rumours.
Chinese.

They describe rumours as information spread on social media without intensive investigation, fact and background checking, and as misleading or false information. They also hypothesize that information not confirmed by official news, i.e., unconfirmed (novel) information with respect to trusted resources, is an indication of rumours.
LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
The paper explores generative document evaluation, document classification, and the topic2sentence task.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, Chinese OntoNotes 4.0, MSRA, Weibo NER, and Resume NER.

They use relative positional encodings \(R_{t-j}\) based on the offset \(t-j\) and compute attention as \(A^{rel}_{t,j}=Q_tK_j^T+Q_tR_{t-j}^T+uK_j^T+vR_{t-j}^T\). Because \(R_{t-j}\) uses sinusoidal functions of the relative offset, it captures distance, and since the sine terms change sign for positive vs. negative offsets while cosine terms do not, it also distinguishes direction.

Yes. On English NER datasets, the adapted Transformer “unveil[s] the new state-of-the-art performance” among models using non-contextualized embeddings, and with ELMo it also achieves the best reported scores in their comparisons.

The state-of-the-art systems mentioned are SEMAFOR, SLING, and Stanford KBP for knowledge extraction/authoring comparison, and Variational Reasoning Network for question answering on MetaQA. Related works also list OpenIE, Memory Network, ATHENA, and PowerAqua.

They evaluated KALM-QA on two datasets: a manually constructed set of general questions based on the 50 logical frames, and the MetaQA dataset. For KALM knowledge authoring, they evaluated on 250 manually constructed sentences adapted from FrameNet exemplar sentences.

The paper contributes: (i) the adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence-to-sequence neural system to Italian event detection and classification in a single-step approach; (ii) an investigation of the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and (iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. It also makes the pre-trained models and scripts publicly available.

The paper uses the best system from EVENTI Subtask B, FBK-HLT (Mirza and Minard, 2014), as its baseline comparison. FBK-HLT is a cascade of two SVM classifiers, one for detection and one for classification, based on rich linguistic features.
Unanswerable

They assign a cluster’s semantic concept label by first disambiguating each word in the cluster to WordNet synsets so that the chosen synsets minimize total distance within the cluster, then generating candidate labels as the union of all hypernyms of those synsets, and finally ranking the candidates by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to the cluster synsets. The selected label is the candidate hypernym with the smallest total distance to the synsets in the cluster.

They discover coherent word clusters by first training domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings and then applying k-means clustering to the embeddings of gender-associated words, running k-means 50 times and keeping the solution with the lowest sum of squared errors. They then label clusters using WordNet and domain-specific embeddings via sense disambiguation, candidate label generation from hypernyms, and ranking labels by distance to the chosen synsets.
Unanswerable
The authors used the centroid of the cluster as a strong baseline label. They also compared Word Intrusion results against a random baseline of 20%.
They generally respond poorly: most data-driven systems rank low in appropriateness. They often produce flirtatious or retaliatory responses, and only Neuralconvo and Cleverbot ever politely refuse.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers. After filtering, 18,826 scores remained.
Yes. The automatically annotated time expressions were evaluated: the customized HeidelTime version achieved an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and fusional.

Yes. The paper discusses morphology broadly, noting limitations related to rich derivational morphology, and the model’s morphological outputs include language-specific morphological tags such as case, number, gender, and person, which are inflectional features.

Gold morphological features are used as the outputs of the morphology function. For all languages, these consist of the token’s lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags, with additional information for some languages such as part-of-speech tags for Turkish.

The datasets used are Semantic Scholar (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, and the Flickr30K and COCO benchmarks.
English.
Unanswerable

For image-sentence matching, they compared against Embedding network, 2WayNet, VSE++, and DSVE-loc. For caption and figure classification, the supervised baselines were the same networks initialized with random weights without FCC training, plus frozen VGG16 weights trained on ImageNet for figure classification. For TQA, they used the baselines from Kembhavi et al. (2017): random, BiDAF, text only (TQA1, based on MemoryNet), text+image (TQA2, VQA), and text+diagrams (TQA3, DSDP-NET).

Yes. The main datasets are scientific publications corpora such as Semantic Scholar and SciGraph, and the TQA corpus is from middle school science curricula, although Wikipedia is noted as a general-purpose source rather than scientific-domain specific.

Their figures and captions come from the Semantic Scholar corpus: from 3.3M downloaded PDFs, they extracted 12.5M figures and captions using PDFFigures2. They also used 82K figures from the intersection of Springer Nature’s SciGraph with Semantic Scholar for some training configurations and supervised baselines.
Unanswerable
No. The abstract states that their model predicts intensity “without leveraging on lexicons.”
The baseline was the Weka baseline. In Table 2, its average Pearson correlation was 0.611 on the development set and 0.648 on the test set.

Their model outperformed the Weka baseline on the development set but underperformed it on the test set. From Table 2, the average Pearson correlation was 0.689 vs. 0.611 on development, and 0.522 vs. 0.648 on test.
The training, validation, and test datasets provided for the EmoInt-2017 shared task were used, consisting of annotated tweets for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger.

They do not give a formal explicit definition, but describe hate speech as promoting the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups and potentially inciting hate crime. Their annotation framework further treats it as text that discriminates against an individual or group of people, and may be offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal.
English, French, and Arabic.
The aspects considered are directness, hostility type, target attribute, target group, and sentiment of the annotator.

The dataset contains a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language plus final comparable corpora of 5,647 English tweets, 4,014 French tweets, and 3,353 Arabic tweets. In total, that is 13,314 tweets in the final comparable corpora, or 13,614 including the pilot corpus.

Semantic Web technologies offer opportunities to improve machine translation by providing semantic disambiguation, better handling of syntactic ambiguity through ontology reasoning, improved named entity recognition/disambiguation and OOV handling via linked knowledge bases, adaptation to non-standard speech using user/context data in RDF, and translation/enrichment of knowledge bases for use in MT models. They are especially valuable because they can provide unseen knowledge about emergent data and enable the combined use of multiple Semantic Web resources such as DBpedia and multilingual linked idiom datasets.

The challenges associated with using Semantic Web technologies in Machine Translation include that applying Semantic Web techniques for disambiguation as pre- or post-editing is tedious to implement for common words and may need to be applied several times, and that using reasoning over ontologies suffers from poor run-time performance that must be minimized before successful MT integration. The paper also notes that Semantic Web methods have mostly been applied only at the semantic analysis step, while broader integration into other translation stages remains limited.

Structural divergence and linguistic properties/features. The paper also lists five additional open challenges: excessive focus on English/European languages and low-resource pairs, cross-domain translation limitations, translating social-network non-standard speech, difficult translation among morphologically rich languages, and mismatch between speech-translation training data and real user speech.

The EEG features used were five statistical features: root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis, and power spectral entropy. After KPCA reduction to 30 dimensions, delta and delta-delta features were also computed, giving a final EEG feature dimension of 90.

The baselines were two state-of-the-art end-to-end ASR models: a Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) model and an RNN encoder-decoder attention model. Both were trained using EEG features or concatenated EEG and acoustic features.

The dataset used was built by the authors using simultaneous speech-EEG recordings, with sentences taken from the first 30 sentences of the USC-TIMIT database. They created dataset A with 10 subjects and 40 dB background noise, and dataset B with 8 subjects and 65 dB background music noise.
No. In Table 3, BERT-Base—the teacher—is more accurate than all LadaBERT variants on every listed task.

Yes. They compare single methods and combinations, and conclude that the combination of matrix factorization and weight pruning is better than single solutions, with LadaBERT’s combination of SVD-based matrix factorization, weight pruning, and knowledge distillation showing superior performance.
MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, SST-2, QQP, and QNLI.
News articles, specifically datasets created from DailyMail news and evaluation on DUC-2002 documents covering various news topics.

They use two datasets created from DailyMail news for training, validation, and testing, and evaluate on the DUC-2002 single document summarization task as well as 500 articles from the DailyMail test set.
Yes. They compare against a neural abstractive baseline (NN-ABS), and report that the word extraction model (NN-WE) consistently outperforms the purely abstractive model.

They focus on commonsense knowledge represented as ontologies of hierarchical data, specifically transitive entailment relations such as IS-A. They also mention augmenting this hierarchical supervision with non-hierarchical knowledge from raw text.

They mean embedding spaces whose geometric structure directly encodes and enforces the ontology’s partial-order constraints, so predictions are automatically internally consistent. In the order-embedding model, this geometry means a supercategory’s embedding is smaller than a subcategory’s embedding in every coordinate.
Yes. They used pre-trained sentiment embeddings learned with a biLSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank.

The models extracted n-gram features (word unigrams and bigrams with TF-IDF), distributed semantic features (300-dimensional word2vec embeddings, with experiments using GloVe and Sent2Vec), and sentiment features. The sentiment features came from lexical-category percentages across 192 Empath categories and pre-trained sentiment embeddings learned with a biLSTM on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank.
Unanswerable
The multimodal resources are AI2D and AI2D-RST. AI2D-RST contains three parts: grouping, connectivity, and discourse structure.
Unanswerable

They are compared through two experiments using graph neural networks on the same diagrams, evaluating crowd-sourced AI2D graphs against expert-annotated AI2D-RST graphs on node classification and graph classification tasks. Performance is measured with accuracy, macro F1, and weighted F1 averaged over 20 runs, with statistical significance tested using the Mann–Whitney U test.
Amazon Mechanical Turk.
Trained experts are the annotators who annotated the AI2D-RST subset of 1000 diagrams using a new multi-layer annotation schema for describing the diagrams.
They opt for two neural model architectures: a Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN) based on bi-directional GRUs and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). They explore both for controversy detection.

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset for baseline comparison, and for cross-temporal, cross-topic, and cross-domain experiments they generate a new dataset based on Wikipedia crawl data. This new dataset is built from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages from 2018 and 2009.

Cross-domain appears to be the hardest setup. Its best F1 score is 0.645, which is lower than cross-topic (best F1 0.753) and cross-temporal (best F1 0.695 in the between-year setting), while the across-annotators section reports agreement correlations rather than a directly comparable difficulty score.

They use weak signal data derived from Wikipedia crawl data: Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages (2018 and 2009) as seed sets, then iteratively crawling “See also,” “References,” and “External links” up to two hops; negative seeds come from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint. Labels are weakly assumed to propagate from controversial seeds to linked pages.
Yes. They compare the semantic/neural approaches to lexical baselines such as TfIdf-SVM and language models, and report that the neural models show a slight advantage over the lexical models.
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising 1.2 billion tokens, were used for training.
About 64 million segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2 billion tokens.

The videos were represented by 1500-dimensional frame-level visual feature vectors extracted from video frames at 1-second intervals, similar to features used for large-scale image classification tasks.

Yes. The experiments used an LSTM-based NMT architecture “implementing the attention mechanism,” specifically the “scaled Luong” attention, and they also mention experimenting with a Transformer model.

The experiments use an established NMT architecture based on LSTMs with attention, specifically the Luong et al. (2017) model. They also experimented with the Transformer model, but its results were worse.

Symbolic rewriting is the rewriting of symbolic terms from one form to another using rewrite rules such as equations. In the paper, it is treated as a formal-to-formal translation task, for example transforming term pairs \(u[\theta(s)]\) to \(u[\theta(t)]\) using a rule \(s=t\), or converting a polynomial from an arbitrary form to a normalized form.

They incorporate expert knowledge by having users specify anchor terms/variables—e.g., domain-specific words like “diabetes,” “insulin,” or “metformin”—and adding a mutual-information term between those anchors and latent factors to the CorEx objective. This anchored objective preserves information about the anchors while still explaining multivariate dependence in the data, thereby seeding topics and imposing semantics on the learned latent factors.
20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 2008 Obesity Challenge dataset.
No. The paper discusses SeededLDA in related work, but the reported experimental comparisons are against unsupervised CorEx and a Naive Bayes baseline, not popular topic models such as LDA.
54.82 overall F1.

Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media. The paper states that its integrated training model (F-Score Driven Model II) achieves a new state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media.
Sina Weibo.
They used a modified labelled Weibo NER corpus for Chinese social media, as in Peng and Dredze (2016). They also used the same unlabeled text from the Sina Weibo service in China.
XNLI (cross-lingual natural language inference), MLDoc (document classification), cross-lingual intent classification, POS tagging, and named entity recognition.
Yes. The paper states that in the few-shot setting, “the gains are more pronounced in low resource languages.”

No. The paper says mBERT is pretrained on monolingual data in 104 languages, and in experiments it is evaluated in both zero-shot and in-language settings rather than being described as fine-tuned separately for each language.

They did not describe a special selection procedure for the full set; the tested languages come from the languages covered by the five benchmark tasks, totaling over 50 languages. The only explicit selection criterion stated is for POS tagging, where they chose 48 languages from the intersection of languages available in Universal Dependencies and those supported by their mNMT model.

They use both automatic and human evaluations. Automatic evaluation uses perplexity and BLEU against human-annotated responses, while human evaluation uses ACUTE-EVAL in a self-chat A/B setting where annotators compare full dialogues for engagingness, interestingness, and humanness.
They state that the cross-lingual models show a “significant gap” and “inferior performance” compared to the other models. No exact numerical amount is given.
They compare against a translation-based Poly-encoder pipeline using the Google Translate API: target-language query → English query → English response → target-language response.
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.
Yes. They automatically created their own contrastive test set from the OpenSubtitles corpus by extracting examples and introducing contrastive translations.

The baselines are standard context-agnostic sentence-level models: a bidirectional RNN with attention trained with Nematus, and a Transformer with parameters identical to Transformer-base in Vaswani et al. (2017). The paper also considers simple concatenation baselines, concat22 for both RNN and Transformer, and concat21 for Transformer.

The experimented context-aware models were, for RNNs: concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, and s-hier-to-2; and for Transformers: concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) model. The paper also states they experiment with additional context on the source side and target side.
English and German.

They use a simple entity linking algorithm that directly links each mention to the entity with the greatest commonness score, where commonness is computed from Wikipedia anchor links as the probability of an entity given the mention string. They also use this commonness score as the confidence of the linking result and apply a heuristic to corefer generic person mentions to more specific mentions within the same document.

They use a fine-grained entity typing architecture that builds context, mention-string, and KB-type representations, where the context is encoded with two layers of BiLSTMs. These representations, plus the entity-linking confidence score, are concatenated and fed into a three-layer MLP for prediction.
They evaluate on two datasets: FIGER (GOLD) and BBN. OntoNotes is mentioned but not used.
1 domain expert.
F1-score is used for evaluation. The paper reports an F1-score of 0.89 for the concept recognition task.
The fine-tuned BERT model achieved an F1-score of 0.89 on the concept recognition dataset, using an 80–20 train/evaluation split.
Yes, the constructed dataset is open sourced; the paper states that the implementation with demo and dataset is available at https://github.com/jitinkrishnan/NASA-SE.

The labeling scheme is a BIO labelling scheme with entity types: abb, grp, syscon, opcon, seterm, event, org, art, cardinal, loc, and mea. These represent, respectively, abbreviations; groups or individuals; system concepts; operational concepts; generic SE terms; event-like information; organizations; artifacts or instruments; numerical values; location-like entities; and measures/features/behaviors.
BERT’s pre-trained cased language model.
The constructed concept recognition dataset contains roughly 3700 annotated sentences at the word-token level.
Precision, recall and F-measure were computed.
The hand-crafted features mentioned are the position of sentence, sentence length, tense, content features, qualifying adjectives, and meta-discourse features.
The study uses classical word2vec word embeddings, with three sentence-representation models explored: averaging word vectors, paragraph vectors (PV-DM), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE).
No. They use existing datasets: the AZ corpus is already AZ-annotated, and the training data comes from the ACL Anthology Reference Corpus and MixedAbs collection.

Sentence embeddings are generated using three explored models: by averaging the word vectors in a sentence, by training paragraph vectors with the PV-DM (paragraph vector) model, and by averaging BAS-specific word embeddings. The averaged word vectors are learned with the classical word2vec algorithm.

Argumentative zoning, also called rhetorical sentence classification, is the process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences. In this paper, it uses seven categories of rhetorical status: aim, contrast, own, background, other, basis, and textual.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites, YouLikeHits and Like4Like, from February to April 2019, created dummy accounts, and recorded the Tweet IDs of tweets posted there for gaining retweets. They then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects, and also collected the authors’ timelines to obtain genuine tweets by the same users.
They compare against state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods—Wu et al. [5] and Rajdev et al. [12]—and feature-concatenation baselines using Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English
They look at freemium blackmarket services, specifically three categories: social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services.
They use English and Chinese for the two-language pretraining setting, and English, French, and Chinese for the three-language pretraining setting.

The decoder is part of a Transformer-based sequence-to-sequence model, and it supports multiple languages. It generates the output sequence autoregressively in the target language by conditioning on the encoder’s vector representations and a specific language tag.

The encoder is based on Transformer and is bidirectional, encoding multilingual input sentences into vector representations with language tag embeddings used to distinguish source and target languages. In pre-training, the encoder is trained to map multilingual sentences into a shared embedding space.

The baselines are CorefNQG, MP-GSN, and XLM for supervised English-English question generation; and for zero-shot cross-lingual question generation they use XLM and pipeline baselines built on XLM, including versions with a translator or Google Translator. For summarization, the baselines are XLM and the same XLM-based pipeline methods.

The human evaluation metrics are a triple pairing task, where judges select the correct prompt-story pairing for groups of three shuffled stories and prompts, and a blind human preference evaluation comparing hierarchical generation from a prompt against generation without a prompt. These were conducted on Amazon Mechanical Turk.
Perplexity and prompt ranking accuracy.

Language-model baselines: non-hierarchical story generation models that do not condition on the prompt, using both the gated convolutional language model (GCNN) of Dauphin et al. (2017) and their additional self-attention mechanism. They also compare against seq2seq baselines using LSTMs, convolutional seq2seq architectures, and Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention; an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models; and a KNN retrieval model that finds the closest prompt in the training set using FASTTEXT TF-IDF vectors and FAISS.
No. The paper uses a fusion mechanism trained on top of a pre-trained seq2seq model, not pre-trained embeddings like BERT.
The premise is generated using the convolutional language model from Dauphin et al. (2017).
Yes. The dataset is collected from Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum, where users submit story premises and other users respond with stories.
The stories are collected from Reddit’s Writing Prompts forum, scraped from three years of prompts and their associated stories using the official Reddit API.
word2vec, fastText, GloVe, Baroni, and SL999.

SICK, STSB, and MRPC (Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus) were used. SICK was used for textual entailment recognition and also for semantic textual similarity, STSB for semantic textual similarity, and MRPC for paraphrase identification.

They compare with DT TEAM, ECNU, BIT, TF-KLD, Multi-Perspective CNN, InferSent, GRAN, and Paragram-Phrase. The abstract also states they outperform the state-of-the-art methods for textual similarity tasks on the STS Benchmark and SICK datasets.
Fleiss’s Kappa.

Yes. They report inter-annotator agreement using Fleiss’s Kappa in Table 3, with values such as 0.23 for aggression, 0.18 for repetition, 0.42 for harmful intent, 0.51 for visibility among peers, and 0.18 for cyberbullying.

The social-network features are five neighborhood-overlap measures between the author and target in the directed following network: downward overlap, upward overlap, inward overlap, outward overlap, and bidirectional overlap. The paper also uses user-based account metrics from author and target profiles: number of friends, number of followers, verified status, and number of tweets posted within six-month timeline snapshots.
The five factors considered are aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance.

Cyberbullying is defined in the document as containing a strict subset of these criteria: aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance. It is also described by prior work as repeated acts of aggression intended to inflict injury or discomfort on a weaker victim.

The output was evaluated objectively for similarity to training data using Phrase Overlap and 4-gram Jaccard similarity, syntactically using the Link Grammar Parser to measure valid linkages and sentence correctness, and subjectively for humor/quality using human judgment. Syntactic accuracy was reported as the percentage of sentences with at least one valid linkage at Null Count 0.
The joke data came from two sources: CrowdTruth and Subreddits.
Inspirational quotes.
They use filters of size 1 × 3 in the convolution layer. Multiple 1 × 3 filters are applied over every row of the k × 3 input matrix.

On FB15k-237, they outperform the closely related state-of-the-art ConvKB by 0.105 MRR points (about 25.1% relative improvement) and 6.1% absolute Hits@10. They also obtain the highest MRR and Hits@10 scores on FB15k-237.
No. The experiments described use only BERT, and the overview states that under the finetune-only strategy they use only single BERT.

They compared single BERT with BERT plus other models: BERT + Bi-LSTM for sequence labeling; BERT + HighwayLSTM, BERT + DenseNet, and an ensembled model for text classification; and BERT + BIMPM, BERT + BIMPM with the first bi-LSTM removed, and BERT + Sim-Transformer for semantic similarity.
CoNLL03, Yahoo Answer Classification Dataset, and Quora-Question-Pair dataset.

They obtained high inter-annotator agreement: in Phase 1, raw agreement was .92/.95/.90 and Cohen’s κ was .90/.93/.88 for scene role/function/construal; in Phase 2, raw agreement was .93/.90/.89 and Cohen’s κ was .92/.88/.88.

They annotated the Chinese translation of The Little Prince manually: after Jieba tokenization, they manually corrected segmentation so potential adpositions were separate tokens, and all annotators jointly identified adposition targets according to their criteria. Three native Mandarin speakers then labeled supersenses in two phases, with 25% of targets annotated cooperatively by all three and the rest annotated independently followed by cooperative adjudication; POS tags and dependency trees from StanfordNLP were stored with the annotations in CoNLL-U-Lex format.
20,287 tokens.

They use the training dataset with five features: ID, title, author, text, and label, containing 20,800-odd samples. They do not use the provided test dataset because it has no labels, and instead randomly select a test set from the training data for evaluation.

The paper explores three text representation models: TF-IDF, CountVectorizer, and Word2Vec, and five fine-tuning models: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Long Short-Term Memory networks (LSTMs), Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM).

The essays are represented with six feature groups: numerical features, language-model probabilities, word-cluster features, topic-model features, part-of-speech tag features, and essay-text features. Concretely, these include counts/readability and error-related measures (e.g., sentences, words, letters, Gunning Fog, difficult/misspelled/duplicate words, low-idf words), probabilities under two trigram language models, binary one-hot vectors over 1,000 word clusters, concatenated LDA topic distributions (30/40/50/60 topics), POS unigram bag-of-words, and bigram bag-of-words with idf weighting.
The evaluation metrics were the challenge’s official error measure E and accuracy. The paper reports stratified 3-fold cross-validation scores for both metrics.
Gradient boosted trees trained with gradient-based one-side sampling, implemented in Microsoft’s Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM), are used as the classification model.

Future work is to investigate possible biases in the process, especially whether the usefulness of LDA topic distributions reflects true modeling power or an artifact of the dataset. The author suggests further analysis and validation, particularly if essay topics are released so validation splits can be made based on topics to test whether LDA is exploiting information leakage.
For reference, the baseline was a tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for the class imbalance. It achieved error \(E = 10.10\) and accuracy \(97.2\).

Sentence alignment quality is evaluated manually on a 5-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with slightly compositional translational equivalence, 3 = partial alignment with compositional translation and additional or missing information, 4 = correct alignment with compositional translation and few additional or missing information, and 5 = correct alignment and fully compositional translation. The corpus is split into three bins by hunalign confidence score, and two annotators each rate 30 items from each bin, with overlap for inter-annotator reliability.

The speech alignment quality is evaluated manually as audio-text alignment on a 3-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment with some words or sentences possibly missing, and 3 = correct alignment, allowing non-spoken syllables at the start or end. Two annotators each rated 30 items from each of three bins defined by hunalign confidence scores, with 10 shared items per bin for inter-annotator reliability.

Yes. The paper explicitly says the selective gate is designed to handle bags with only one sentence, where selective attention is ineffective, by assigning a low gating value to wrongly labeled instances and preventing noisy representations from being propagated.

No. The paper discusses one-sentence bags in the introduction and presents a four-example case study comparing their selective gate framework, but it does not show a case-study example explicitly demonstrating that a one-sentence bag is correctly handled by their method in contrast to selective attention.

For semantic relatedness, their CharCNN+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN reaches 0.851 Pearson correlation, outperforming the best listed traditional baseline (0.828) by 0.023. For textual entailment, it reaches 0.842 accuracy, outperforming the best listed traditional baseline (0.836) by 0.006.
They evaluate on the standard SICK (Sentences Involving Compositional Knowledge) dataset, which contains about 10,000 English sentence pairs annotated for semantic relatedness and textual entailment.

No. They report only end-task results for models such as WordEmbedding+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN and CharCNN+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN, and comparisons like Tree LSTM vs Sequence LSTM with or without CNN, but no separate evaluation of the learned representations before passing them to the CNN layer.
Unanswerable
SRE04/05/06/08/10/MIXER6, Switchboard (LDC98S75/LDC99S79/LDC2002S06/LDC2001S13/LDC2004S07), Voxceleb 1/2, Fisher + Switchboard I, and Callhome + Callfriend were used.
Unanswerable
Yes. They increase frequencies of co-occurring expressions from the same similarity set so the algorithm can “see” similarities between different multiword expressions with the same component word.

They explore several domains: European Union political/economic texts (Europarl and JRC-Acquis), computational linguistics (ACL Anthology), machine learning/neural information processing (NIPS papers), Russian banking texts, and a specialized Islam information portal. They also note that EuroVoc covers economic and political domains.
WordNet, EuroVoc, and RuThes.

They define hate speech as language used to express hatred toward a targeted group or intended to be derogatory, humiliating, or insulting to members of that group. In extreme cases, it may also threaten or incite violence, but they do not limit the definition only to such cases.
A logistic regression with L2 regularization in a one-versus-rest framework.
33,458 Twitter users.
85.4 million tweets.

SST-2. On this sentiment classification task, the paper reports that for the large variants of BERT and RoBERTa, quality consistently increases when freezing 12–16 layers, indicating that fine-tuning all layers hurts performance.
Yes, they test RoBERTa LARGE.

It mitigates the bias pattern by making hypothesis artifacts balanced across labels through sample reweighting. Specifically, it estimates \(P(y \mid h)\) via K-fold cross-prediction and trains with weights \(w=\frac{1}{(1-\epsilon)P(y\mid h)+\epsilon}\), normalized so the total weight for each label is the same, which is equivalent to training on an artifact-balanced distribution.
They use SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI, and SICK for cross-dataset testing. Specifically, they train and validate on SNLI and MultiNLI, and test on SICK and JOCI.

The entropy strategy was the most helpful. It reached optimal performance the quickest for both machine learning models (LR and RF), while other strategies showed no clear advantage or were not useful for CNN.
3,685,984 unique tweets.
10K sentences and 250K tokens.

They explored neural network-based POS taggers with embeddings over words plus optional prefixes, suffixes, and character-level CNN features; encoding was done with either a feedforward network using a context window or a bidirectional LSTM. For prediction, they tested either a softmax layer with greedy decoding or a CRF layer with Viterbi decoding.

The rule-based model evaluated was the rule-based tagger by Rashel et al. [15]. It tags named entities and multi-word expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd [16] for the remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted rules for post-processing ambiguous tags.
The task used the IDN Tagged Corpus proposed in [12], containing 10K sentences and 250K manually tagged tokens.
They use parts of Chinese Wikipedia articles, yielding 11,529,432 segmented words for training. They also collect stroke information for 20,402 characters.
No. They evaluate the embeddings only on two test tasks: word similarity and word analogy.
Unanswerable

The issues identified are that the model lacked flexibility on out-of-vocabulary words and, for unknown words, it assigned a zero vector, which hurt test performance. The paper also notes that this lack of flexibility could be seen as an advantage because the model recognized the mismatch of an incorrect word with its class.

Yes. The evaluation reports results for “POS and morph classes” versus “Only POS classes,” with precision, recall, and F1 scores, so morphology is evaluated as part of the combined POS-and-morph tagging task.

It extends ENAMEX by using a more complex named-entity scheme that, beyond people, locations, and organizations, can also detect numerical types such as percentages (NUMEX) and dates (TIMEX). In the proposed Greek NER model, the implemented label set consists of four classes: location (LOC), organization (ORG), person (PERSON), and facility (FAC).
The extracted morphological features are gender, number, and case.
Yes. The reported corpus and results are based on Twitter data from users who announced their pregnancy on Twitter, and no non-English datasets are mentioned.

The dataset was a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of more than 400 million publicly available tweets by more than 100,000 users who had announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators labeled users as bot, non-bot, or unavailable based on their publicly available Twitter sites, with bots broadly defined as users not appearing to post personal information and unavailable users being those whose sites could not be viewed due to privacy changes, removal, or suspension. Inter-annotator agreement on 1,000 overlapping annotations was κ = 0.93, and after resolving disagreements, 413 (4%) users were bots, 7,849 (75.35%) were non-bots, and 2,069 (19.9%) were unavailable.

By extending the existing political bot detection system with additional features derived from health-related data and training a statistical machine learning classifier on them. The paper customizes it by using the Botometer score as a feature in a gradient boosting classifier and adding features such as tweet diversity, URL score, mean daily posts, topics, mean post length, profile picture faces, and presence of a person’s name in the username.
Using social media data for health-related research.
No. The 2000 QA tuples are sourced from Twitter customer support conversations, but the paper says its conclusions “don’t have to be limited to this field.”
bert-base-multilingual-cased
The corpus contains 2000 QA tuples.
They used a customer support dataset collected by Hori and Hori [12] from Twitter, containing 2000 question-answer tuples.

The boosting technique used was a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), described as fast gradient boosting on decision trees. It combined predictions from the base classifiers with TF-IDF and QIEF features.
Yes. They compared different BERT embeddings: the original general BERT and the domain-specific BioBERT, and found BioBERT performed better.
The improved dataset (PICONET) contains 96,328 sequences. This is the sum of the category counts in Table 1: I 22,818, IO 7, IP 337, O 10,994, P 30,106, PO 13, and NEGATIVE 32,053.

The new dataset, PICONET, was collected by retrieving 363,078 structured PubMed abstracts on April 11, 2019 using the filters Clinical Trial, Humans, and English, then lemmatizing section headings and carefully selecting headings that uniquely represented Population, Intervention, and Outcome. The authors manually checked samples of candidate headings, kept full abstract sections rather than splitting them into sentences, created multi-labels for combined headings such as “population and intervention,” added negative examples from headings like “aim,” and cleaned the data with language identification, missing-value removal, unicode cleaning, and filtering sequences to 5–200 words.
Unanswerable

Previous datasets are described as having redundancy and ambiguity. In particular, a keyword-based approach could label sections that were not purely about Population, Intervention, or Outcome and therefore were poor candidates, and the dataset of Jin and Szolovits (2018) split multi-sentence labeled sections into individual sentences, which the paper argues can produce poor candidates and lose feature-rich context.
Unanswerable

They explore four agreement relation types: subject-verb agreement, noun-determiner agreement, noun-attributive adjective agreement, and subject-predicate adjective agreement. They study these across four features: number, gender, case, and person.
Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, and TREC.
Their approach is compared against TextFooler as the baseline attack, and it is evaluated on three text classification models: word-LSTM, word-CNN, and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier.
0.008 F1 points.
Buschmeier, Cimiano, and Klinger (2014), with precision 0.82, recall 0.69, and F1 0.744.
The baseline models are “Baseline: All Sarcasm” and “Baseline: Random.” In Table 5, “All Sarcasm” predicts all instances as sarcastic, and “Random” is the random baseline.
The explored domains are Twitter and Amazon product reviews.

The training data came from two domains: Twitter and Amazon product reviews. The Amazon data was the publicly available sarcasm corpus developed by Filatova (2012), and the Twitter data consisted of tweets collected in February and March 2016 containing exactly one trailing hashtag: “#sarcasm” for sarcastic tweets, or one of “#happiness,” “#sadness,” “#anger,” “#surprise,” “#fear,” and “#disgust” for non-sarcastic tweets.
LSTM and GRU with Attention was the best model, with 0.89 macro-F1 and 0.88 weighted-average F1. Its Offensive-class scores were precision 0.96, recall 0.68, and F1 0.77.

The dataset was tested with classical models and deep learning models. The classical models were SVM with linear kernel, SVM with RBF kernel, Stochastic Gradient Descent Classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes. The deep learning models were Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.
LSTM and GRU with Attention best performs on the offensive language identification task. It achieved the highest macro-F1 (0.89) and is described as the best model trained for OGTD.
No. The annotations were done by a team of three volunteers using LightTag, not via crowdsourcing.
3 volunteers.
No. The OGTD v1.0 dataset is imbalanced, containing 1,401 offensive tweets and 3,378 not offensive tweets, with over 29% offensive content.

They experiment on five classical models—linear SVM, RBF SVM, Stochastic Gradient Descent classifier, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes—and six deep learning models: Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.
Unanswerable
910 reviews.
3 annotators.

The sentence construction component is the GF Grammar Encoder, which identifies appropriate GF rules to build a GF grammar for a sentence from its recognized structure and components. It assigns an initial GF rule to each sentence structure, then constructs each parameter by selecting extended GF rules in top-to-bottom order under the constraints that the resulting type matches the required type and that the input/output types are predetermined, making rule selection unambiguous. It then uses these rules and components to generate GF constructors for sentence parts and corresponding operators for the grammar.

The two use cases are: generating natural language descriptions for annotated ontologies/workflows, including the Phylotastic project and a people ontology; and creating an intermediate representation for Wikipedia pages that can be translated, then regenerating English and evaluating it against the original.

Yes. They analyze the structure of the learned language vectors and show that these representations capture similarity and genetic relationships between languages, including hierarchical clustering of Germanic languages.

A standard stacked character-based LSTM with two layers, followed by a hidden layer and a final softmax output layer. It incorporates language embedding vectors concatenated to the LSTM inputs at each time step and to the hidden layer before the softmax.

They show genetic relationships by using hierarchical clustering of the learned language vectors. For example, Figure 5 clusters Germanic languages in a way that closely matches established genetic relationships in that family.

No. The paper says MagiCoder was developed to support pharmacovigilance supervisors and claims it reduces the required time, but the reported evaluation tested performance by comparing automatic encoding against manually validated reports from VigiSegn, not by testing time savings on real pharmacologists.
No explicit hypothesis is offered. The authors only report that MagiCoder behaves very well on very short and short descriptions, while performances drastically decrease on very long descriptions.

The MagiCoder algorithm has five phases: definition of ad hoc data structures, preprocessing of the original text, word-by-word linear scan of the description with a voting task, weights calculation, and sorting of voted terms with release of the winning terms. In preprocessing it performs tokenization, stemming, and elimination of irrelevant words; in the scan each word votes for LLTs it belongs to, storing matching information. Then it weights recognized terms, prunes and orders them, and finally releases a small set of winning MedDRA terms.

The system preprocesses the narrative into an array of words, builds hash-based meta-dictionaries mapping each dictionary word or stem to the set of MedDRA terms containing it, and then performs a single word-by-word scan of the description, voting only the terms associated with each encountered word and storing positions in auxiliary constant-time update structures. Building the meta-dictionaries takes O(km) and is done only when the dictionary changes, while the narrative is processed by one linear scan and subsequent phases operate on the voted terms subset, so the method is constructed to be linear in the size of the narrative input and the terminology.

They conclude that a more complete picture of the aspects and mechanisms of personal recovery in bipolar disorder should be drawn from large-scale first-person social media accounts, complementing prior qualitative interviews and questionnaires. They also conclude that recovery-oriented health service strategies should be informed by diverse voices, since previous recovery research has been largely limited to English-speaking Western participants and may not fully capture broader cultural perspectives.
Unanswerable

The analysis used exploratory quantitative comparative analysis with Wmatrix for keywords and key concepts, an iterative corpus-linguistic approach with a recovery-term dictionary plus semantic tagging using USAS, and distributional semantics to find co-occurring terms. It also considered unsupervised topic modelling via DLATK, and emotion and sentiment analysis using supervised machine-learning methods, including multilingual embedding-based transfer between languages.
No. The paper states that they will not ask the Twitter and Reddit account holders included in their datasets for consent.

Twitter and Reddit users with bipolar disorder will be identified automatically via self-reported diagnosis statements, such as “I was diagnosed with BD-I last week,” by extending the diagnosis patterns and terms for BD from Cohan et al. (2018). Relevant blogs will be manually identified, and their authors will be contacted to obtain informed consent for using their texts.
The training/testing data comes from CCPC1.0. For CI, the paper uses the top frequent 121 forms from CCPC1.0, constituting 80% of the corpus.
Jueju and Lvshi (the two major forms of SHI), as well as 121 major forms (Cipai) of CI. Specifically, Jueju and Lvshi include Wuyan Jueju, Qiyan Jueju, Wuyan Lvshi, and Qiyan Lvshi.

They compare with previous work on reducing OOV rates, specifically Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch (2015b) and Lee, Cho, and Hofmann (2016). The related work also situates their approach against Luong and Manning (2016), Jean et al. (2014), Mi, Wang, and Ittycheriah (2016), and morphology-aware MT work such as Koehn and Hoang (2007), Chahuneau et al. (2013), Tran, Bisazza, and Monz (2015), Sennrich and Haddow (2016), and Tamchyna, Marco, and Fraser (2017).
Yes. They use the word2vec tool and train both SG and CBOW models.

Quality is measured by evaluation tasks on the learned word vectors: word similarity, synonym selection, and word analogy. For word similarity, it is measured by the Pearson correlation between human similarity judgments and cosine distances in vector space; for synonym selection and analogy, it is measured by choosing words with the highest cosine similarity.
No. They report results on both KPTimes and JPTimes, and both datasets are newspaper article datasets.
The news texts were collected by crawling selected online news websites, primarily the New York Times. A secondary source was also used: the Japan Times.
FirstPhrase, which extracts the first N keyphrase candidates from a document.

Two generative neural keyphrase generation models were trained: CopySci, trained on scientific abstracts (KP20k), and CopyNews, trained on newspaper articles (KPTimes). Both use the same CopyRNN encoder-decoder architecture with a copying mechanism.

The editors’ annotations are expert, manually curated tags produced in a semi-automatic process where editors revise algorithm-suggested tags and add new ones, unlike existing datasets that mostly use non-expert annotations from authors or readers. Reader annotations in existing news datasets are largely extractive with very low missing-keyphrase rates, while author annotations in scholarly datasets can be less constrained and include rare variants or misspellings.
Unanswerable
Yes. They use neural word vectors learned with GloVe for the context words.
The model has one hidden layer.
They use F-measure (%) as the evaluation metric. The paper also mentions “accuracy in terms of F-measure.”
They used four datasets: DUC-2001, Inspec, the NUS Keyphrase Corpus, and the ICSI Meeting Corpus.
27.53 on DUC, 27.01 on Inspec, 4.30 on ICSI, and 9.10 on NUS.
The paper compares its system against SingleRank and Topical PageRank, which are presented as the existing strong models. It reports that WikiRank improves over both on all four corpora.

They do not directly verify correctness; they assume the ART teacher’s encoder-decoder attentions provide useful word alignment information. The paper justifies this by observation: “good” attentions are usually considered to lead to accurate translation, and in their case studies the ART attentions cover source tokens better while poor NART attentions correlate with missing or wrong words.

The document says the autoregressive ART model suffers from high inference latency because it must generate tokens sequentially during decoding. It also states the proposed NART model is one order of magnitude faster in inference than a strong LSTM-based ART baseline.
BLEU score is used to measure translation accuracy.
Yes. Besides WMT14, the model was also evaluated on the IWSLT14 German-to-English dataset.
Yes. The experiments and results are reported not only on IWSLT14 German-to-English, but also on WMT14 English-to-German and reversed WMT14 German-to-English.

The most discriminating features are average, maximum, minimum, and standard deviation, with the highest information gain obtained by average-based features. Individually, standard deviation features achieve the highest accuracy (69.2%), and their combination with average features reaches 70.8%, while probability and proportionality features have low information gain.

On the alternate dataset (the DSLCC development set), the three representations obtain comparative results, supporting the robustness of the low dimensionality representation. LDR achieves 99.9/99.9 on Bulgarian/Macedonian, 84.7 and 88.0 on Spain/Argentina Spanish, 87.4 and 90.0 on Portugal/Brazilian Portuguese, 78.0/85.8/86.4 on Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian, 99.4/99.2 on Indonesian/Malay, 99.8/99.3 on Czech/Slovak, and 99.9 on Other languages.

No. The paper states that extracting answer spans from the BioASQ training data “is not perfect” and can produce false negatives because “a sentence answers the question, but the exact string used is not in the synonym list,” but it does not report the coverage of answers being substrings.
Unanswerable

A neural tensor network (NTN) is used as the baseline event-embedding model, and sentiment prediction uses a softmax binary classifier. For script event prediction, the paper uses the SGNN framework with their enhanced event embeddings as input.

It is evaluated on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset, where given an existing event context, the system chooses the most reasonable subsequent event from a candidate list. The paper follows Li et al. (2018a) and uses the SGNN framework, replacing its input event embeddings with the proposed intent- and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings.

The paper uses the hard similarity dataset from Weber et al. (2018) with 230 event pairs and an extended version with 1,000 event pairs, the transitive sentence similarity dataset from Kartsaklis and Sadrzadeh (2014) with 108 pairs of transitive sentences, and the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset for script event prediction. For stock market prediction, it uses an S&P 500 index prediction dataset.

No. In the experiments, they compare Char2Vec against SGNS, a Char2Vec variant without attention (C2V-NO-ATT), PPMI-SVD, and in morphological analysis also Porter Stemmer and Morfessor; fastText is not mentioned.

Yes. The paper says English is morphologically impoverished and that this is unfortunate for the model, because it performs better on words with richer morphology; they therefore expect it to perform even better for morphologically richer languages such as Turkish and German.
English.

The embeddings are evaluated by ranking word pairs according to the cosine similarity of their word vectors and then measuring the correlation with human similarity judgments using Spearman’s ρ. This comparison is done on WordSim353, the MEN test split, and the Rare Word dataset.
15.6/16.5 (accuracy/NDCG) on MCTest-150 and 7.3/4.6 on MCTest-500.

They compared to Addition, Addition-proj, NR (Neural Reasoner), and AR (Attentive Reader). Addition and Addition-proj are simple distributed-representation baselines, while NR and AR are prior deep neural network QA systems.
The baseline is a one-stage RNN system. Its RNN structure is the same as the last stage, containing a 2-layer BLSTM, and it is directly trained to recognize dialect category.

The proposed multi-stage systems outperform the baseline, with the two-stage BLSTM system achieving the best accuracy: 88.88% overall, 87.72% for utterances ≤3s, and 90.04% for >3s, compared to the baseline’s 78.85%, 77.60%, and 80.10%; the three-stage BLSTM gets 87.24%, 85.52%, and 88.96%. They report about a 10% relative ACC improvement over the baseline on every task, with especially strong performance on long-duration speech and best overall robustness from the two-stage system.
Ningxia, Hefei, Sichuan, Shanxi, Changsha, Hebei, Nanchang, Shanghai, Kekka, and Fujian.
Transformer models using Tensor2Tensor, with the final NMT system using the BIG setup (and BASE for exploratory experiments).
Unanswerable
It entirely relies on neural models and does not use any external NLP tools, spell checkers, or hand-crafted confusion sets.
Unanswerable
There is no clear winner according to human evaluation. MODULAR is better at reducing bias, while CONCURRENT is more fluent and preserves meaning better.

The edits were a Wiki community effort: the corpus consists of sentences pre- and post-neutralization by English Wikipedia editors. The paper says these revisions were crawled from Wikipedia edits where editors provided NPOV-related justification, not produced by trained annotators.

Subjective text is automatically neutralized by treating the task as translating a biased source sentence into a target sentence with similar meaning but with subjective bias removed. The paper proposes two encoder-decoder methods: MODULAR, which first uses a BERT-based classifier to detect subjective words and then edits the sentence with an LSTM decoder guided by a join embedding, and CONCURRENT, which uses a BERT encoder to identify subjective words as part of generation.
The investigated task is American Sign Language recognition at the word level. The paper uses the GMU-ASL51 multimodal dataset with 51 word-level sign classes, using RGB video and/or skeletal data.
Unanswerable
